Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after watching a show or scrolling through my phone, but then hours would pass. When I did study, it was usually late at night when I suddenly felt guilty about not doing anything. I'd open my laptop, look at the materials, but my brain wasn't really absorbing much. It was more like going through the motions than actual organized studying.
Q2: My pattern was completely random and mostly driven by panic. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize a deadline was approaching and try to cram. Regular times? That wasn't me at all. Some weeks I'd study a bit, other weeks nothing. It really depended on whether something urgent was happening or if I felt particularly bad about falling behind.
Q3: Usually it was because I got an email notification about something due soon, or I saw a classmate mention an assignment in our group chat. Sometimes I logged in just to check if I'd missed something important, which I usually had. It was rarely because I was genuinely eager to learn—more like damage control to see how far behind I'd fallen.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks early in the semester where I tried to check in regularly, but that fell apart quickly. Life just got in the way, and I found other things more interesting or less stressful. The LMS never became a habit for me—it always felt like a chore I was avoiding.
Q5: The videos were probably the most useful because I'm a visual learner and I could watch them at my own pace. When I actually sat down to watch them, things made more sense than just reading text. I liked being able to pause and rewind. The discussion boards were okay too when people actually posted helpful explanations, though I rarely contributed myself.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and the extra practice materials. They felt overwhelming and I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The discussion posts too—I'd read a few but participating felt pointless when I was already so behind. Why bother engaging when I barely understood what was going on?
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be graded on or if it was short enough that I could finish it without losing focus. If something took too long or seemed too complicated, I'd lose interest fast. I needed to see an immediate payoff, otherwise my brain just checked out.
Q8: Optional activities might as well not have existed for me. If it wasn't required, I wasn't doing it—I could barely keep up with the required stuff. Required activities at least had some pressure attached, even if I still put them off. Optional ones just felt like extra weight I couldn't carry.
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download lecture slides or take screenshots of important stuff and look at them on my phone later, away from the LMS. I also used YouTube to find videos explaining concepts in different ways because sometimes the course materials didn't click for me. My notes were scattered across random notebooks and phone apps.
Q10: There were times I'd be lying in bed reviewing downloaded slides or rewatching videos I'd saved, and none of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd be thinking through problems in my head while doing other things. The LMS only sees when you're logged in, not when you're actually processing information in your own way.
Q11: Definitely. I'd watch a video once on the LMS, then if I found it helpful, I'd download it or find it on YouTube to watch again later without logging in. Same with PDFs—I'd download them and read them offline when I felt like it, which wasn't often, but still. The LMS didn't capture that.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just crashed for me. I was stressed about multiple courses, not sleeping well, and I started feeling like I was too far behind to catch up anyway. That feeling of being overwhelmed made me avoid the course even more. It became this cycle where avoiding it made me more anxious, which made me avoid it more.
Q13: Absolutely. When I had assignments due in other classes or when I was just exhausted from everything, the LMS was the first thing I dropped. I'd tell myself I'd catch up later, but the fatigue and competing deadlines meant I never really did. Confusion about the material also made me not want to engage because it felt pointless.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was physically present on the platform but mentally checked out. It was like my body was going through the motions while my brain was worrying about other things or just blank.
Q15: All the time. I knew assignments were important but I'd keep pushing them off because they felt too hard or I didn't know where to start. The longer I waited, the more overwhelming they seemed, so I'd avoid them even more. It's like I was paralyzed by not knowing how to begin, so I just... didn't.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore the course until a deadline was breathing down my neck, then scramble to submit something, anything. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and was dead otherwise. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I functioned.
Q17: I honestly didn't pay much attention to feedback because by the time I got it, I'd already mentally moved on or was panicking about the next thing. When I did read it, sometimes it was helpful, but often it just made me feel worse about how poorly I was doing. It didn't really change my behavior.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid and I struggled to keep up with the pace. Once I fell behind, it felt impossible to catch up because everything built on previous material. If there had been more flexibility or check-in points, maybe I would've stayed more engaged. But it felt like the train left and I was just watching it go.
Q19: The weekly modules were hard because missing one meant the next one made less sense. That made it easy to spiral. On the other hand, having videos was good for my visual learning style. But the sheer amount of content and the strict deadlines made engagement harder. I needed more breathing room.
Q20: I think sometimes you're trying to figure things out, clicking around, watching videos, but you're not confident enough to submit work. Or maybe you're anxious about getting a bad grade so you keep reviewing but never actually turn anything in. High activity doesn't mean you feel ready to show what you've learned.
Q21: That wasn't really me since I had low activity AND didn't perform well. But I can see how someone might study effectively offline with textbooks or tutors and just use the LMS minimally for submissions. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens, so low activity doesn't always mean low effort.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just mindlessly scrolling. They miss the mental engagement, the offline studying, the anxiety that keeps you from participating. It's like seeing footprints without knowing where the person was actually headed.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in consistent effort, not just showing up or clicking through things. It's when you're curious and want to understand, not just survive. For me, I was never really engaged this semester—I was just trying to stay afloat and mostly failing at that.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling internally or how much I actually wanted to do better but couldn't get myself to act. It wouldn't show the guilt I felt every time I avoided logging in, or the surprise I felt when things got as bad as they did. My emotional experience of this course was completely invisible to any data system.
